title: Forceable signals have incorrect value immediately after using vpi_put_value with vpiNoDelay
body:
When issuing a `vpi_put_value` with `vpiNoDelay` to a _non-forceable_ signal, the signal immediately receives the new, correct value. However, for a _forceable_ signal, even if no forcing functionality is used, the behavior is different, because the `__VforceRd` signal only gets updated after another `eval`.

To trigger the discrepancy, change
```
reg          onebit          /*verilator public_flat_rw @(posedge clk) */;
```
to
```
reg          onebit          /*verilator public_flat_rw @(posedge clk) */ /*verilator forceable*/;
```
in `t_vpi_var.v`, causing it to fail. A more complete version, intended to be submitted as a PR for regression testing once this issue is fixed, can be found [here](https://github.com/Christian-Hecken/verilator/commit/98247350d69c7c3257af9e578403f64d9a8671fb).

I have [conducted some tests ](https://github.com/Christian-Hecken/verilator/commit/afce78e4562a4a5bec8f5af4a1215fa0ee77c220) in both Icarus and Xcelium, and both read the correct, updated value immediately when a `vpiNoDelay` is issued. I tested it with a DPI function, wrapped inside a non-inlined task as [recommended in the documentation](https://verilator.org/guide/latest/connecting.html#dpi-imports-that-access-signals), a VPI system function, and a delayed VPI callback. For comparison, I also tested a VPI callback with `vpiInertialDelay`, and that one behaves consistently across Verilator and the other simulators.

| Time | Signal                                      | Verilator | Icarus  | Xcelium |
|------|---------------------------------------------|-----------|---------|---------|
|  0   | onebit_dpi_import_nodelay                   |     0     |   N/A   |   N/A   |
|  0   | onebit_vpi_sysfun_nodelay                   |     0     |    1    |    1    |
|  0   | onebit_vpi_delayed_callback_vpiNoDelay      |     0     |    0    |    0    |
|  0   | onebit_vpi_immediate_callback_inertialdelay |     0     |    0    |    0    |
|------|---------------------------------------------|-----------|---------|---------|
|  1   | onebit_dpi_import_nodelay                   |     1     |   N/A   |   N/A   |
|  1   | onebit_vpi_sysfun_nodelay                   |     1     |    1    |    1    |
|  1   | onebit_vpi_delayed_callback_vpiNoDelay      |     0     |    1    |    1    |
|  1   | onebit_vpi_immediate_callback_inertialdelay |     1     |    1    |    1    |
|------|---------------------------------------------|-----------|---------|---------|
|  2   | onebit_dpi_import_nodelay                   |     1     |   N/A   |   N/A   |
|  2   | onebit_vpi_sysfun_nodelay                   |     1     |    1    |    1    |
|  2   | onebit_vpi_delayed_callback_vpiNoDelay      |     1     |    1    |    1    |
|  2   | onebit_vpi_immediate_callback_inertialdelay |     1     |    1    |    1    |

[Verilator's documentation states](https://verilator.org/guide/latest/connecting.html#dpi-imports-that-access-signals) the following about the behavior of signal values when using VPI:
> signal values that are changed by the VPI will not immediately propagate their values, instead the top level header file’s `eval()` method must be called.

However, this only warns about signal _propagation_, not the signal value itself. As seen in `t_vpi_var` and other tests, there are several use cases where tests rely on the signal that was set using `vpi_put_value` updating _immediately_.

Currently, a `vpi_get_value` immediately following a `vpi_put_value` with `vpiNoDelay` sees the correct, updated value already without an `eval`, because the VPI implementation in #6704 does not use the `__VforceRd` flag, but constructs the forced value from the `__VforceEn` and `__VforceVal`. I am currently working on #6705, and the new implementation would use the actual `__VforceRd` signal, thus additionally breaking `vpi_get_value` in the above example.

To move forward with the implementation, I would appreciate some direction on whether the current behavior should be maintained, or if the VPI functions should be updated to behave consistently with both forceable and non-forceable signals. For the latter option, I have considered replacing the `__VforceRd` signal with a function that evaluates it based on `__VforceEn` and `__VforceVal`, but I suspect this would have a significant performance impact.
code_blocks:
reg          onebit          /*verilator public_flat_rw @(posedge clk) */;
reg          onebit          /*verilator public_flat_rw @(posedge clk) */ /*verilator forceable*/;
